summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Mass Extinctions & Evolution
summary_sections.content_html:
Mass extinctions are catastrophic events where a significant percentage of all species go extinct in a geologically short time.

  • Big Five mass extinctions:
    1. End-Ordovician (~444 Ma): 85% of species lost; glaciation
    2. Late Devonian (~372 Ma): 75%; ocean anoxia
    3. End-Permian (~252 Ma): ~96% of species; 'The Great Dying'; volcanic activity
    4. End-Triassic (~201 Ma): ~80%; volcanic CO₂ and warming
    5. End-Cretaceous (~66 Ma): ~76%; asteroid impact + volcanism
  • After each mass extinction, surviving species diversify to fill empty niches (adaptive radiation).
  • Example: mammals diversified rapidly after the end-Cretaceous extinction that eliminated non-avian dinosaurs.
  • Some scientists argue we are in a sixth mass extinction caused by human activities.
